clean_story:
Sensory fragments. Assembled worlds.

The brain doesn’t record reality—it builds it, like a sculptor carving order from noise. "Reality" here means the seamless experience you assume is "out there," but is actually a controlled hallucination, stitched from incomplete data.

Your eyes detect light, but your brain fills gaps: blind spots vanish, blurry edges sharpen. Touch a table; your skin senses pressure, yet your mind insists it’s "solid wood," ignoring the 99.9% empty space between atoms. Even time is edited—your brain delays sound ~80ms to sync with slower visual signals, so thunder and lightning feel simultaneous.

Mistakes reveal the process. Stroke patients with hemineglect ignore half their world—their brain literally erases left-side objects from perception. VR experiments show people accept floating virtual hands as "theirs" in seconds, proving reality is whatever the brain expects to fit.

Three related subjects are predictive processing, sensory substitution devices, and how dreams hijack perception.
